title: The Book of MM — Mental Model Quiz
source: The Book of MM by Eric Jorgenson (2026)

questions.question: How does MM measure whether his day was successful?
questions.short_answer: By how many useful things he got done.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM frames success in utilitarian terms: he wakes up each morning asking 'How can I be useful today?' He thinks about impact mathematically — the number of people helped multiplied by how much help each person received equals total utility. This keeps him focused on genuine output rather than appearances, status, or wealth. A useful life, in his view, is one that contributes more to the world than it consumes.
questions.section: Living a Purposeful Life — Be Useful

questions.question: According to MM, what is the formula for evaluating whether a product is worth building?
questions.short_answer: Utility improvement over the current state of the art, multiplied by how many people it would affect.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM applies a mathematical lens to product decisions. He multiplies the degree of improvement a product offers over what already exists by the number of people it impacts. This means a product that makes a huge difference to a small group is just as valuable as one that makes a small difference for a massive audience. It's a deliberate antidote to hype — the question is always: is this genuinely, measurably useful?
questions.section: Living a Purposeful Life — Be Useful

questions.question: What were the five areas MM identified in college as having the biggest positive impact on humanity's future?
questions.short_answer: The internet, sustainable energy, space exploration, artificial intelligence, and genetic rewriting.
questions.elaborated_answer: While at college, MM would think about which areas would most shape humanity's future. He landed on five: the internet, sustainable energy transition, space exploration/multi-planetary life, artificial intelligence, and rewriting genetics. He later pursued three directly — internet (Zip2/PayPal), sustainable energy (Tesla), and space (SpaceX). The last two — AI and genetics — he considered double-edged swords, uncertain in their net benefit.
questions.section: Living a Purposeful Life — Fight for the Future

questions.question: How does MM think about the future when making decisions?
questions.short_answer: As a branching stream of probabilities — actions taken now shift which futures become more or less likely.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM doesn't see the future as fixed. He visualizes it as a tree of branching probabilities, where present actions can accelerate certain paths, slow others, or introduce entirely new ones. This probabilistic mindset allows him to reason strategically: instead of asking 'what will happen?', he asks 'what actions increase the probability of a good future?' This frames every major decision as a move in a long, high-stakes probability game.
questions.section: Living a Purposeful Life — Fight for the Future

questions.question: What is the single most important entrepreneurial trait MM identifies?
questions.short_answer: An obsessive nature about the quality of the product.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM believes that while successful entrepreneurs come in all forms, the one trait worth focusing on is product obsession. He argues that if you're obsessively focused on quality, you'll keep improving the product even when others would settle. He also links obsession to motivation: if you genuinely love what you're building, you'll think about it even when you're not working — giving you a compounding advantage over those who merely tolerate their work.
questions.section: Living a Purposeful Life — Obsess for Success

questions.question: What does MM say is the right starting point for building a company — and what should you avoid?
questions.short_answer: Start from 'what useful thing needs to exist in the world' — not from wanting to be an entrepreneur or make money.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM warns against starting a company as a vehicle for wealth or identity. Instead, the right starting point is identifying something genuinely needed in the world that doesn't yet exist, at the intersection of your abilities and genuine interests. He argues that money follows utility — a well-functioning economy naturally rewards the creation of useful things. If you build something useful, the financial reward is an output, not a goal.
questions.section: Living a Purposeful Life — Obsess for Success

questions.principle: Start Before the World Is Ready
questions.question: What famous survey does MM reference to illustrate that consumers can't predict demand for radically new products?
questions.short_answer: A 1946–1948 survey in which 96% of respondents said they would never buy a television.
questions.elaborated_answer: When building Tesla, MM heard zero people say they wanted an electric car. He uses the early TV survey — where 96% said 'no' to TVs — as evidence that people cannot evaluate products that are genuinely outside their current frame of reference. Waiting for consumer validation before building something truly new is therefore a category error. If you need encouragement from the market to start, MM argues, you probably shouldn't start.
questions.section: Living a Purposeful Life — Start Before the World Is Ready

questions.question: What mindset does MM say underlies most morally questionable or counterproductive behavior in business?
questions.short_answer: A zero-sum mindset — the belief that the only way to gain is by taking from others.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM argues that the economy is fundamentally a positive-sum, 'grow the pie' system. Economic output per person has grown dramatically over time, far outpacing population growth. People who operate with a zero-sum worldview — believing wealth is fixed and must be seized from others — often act destructively without realizing it. The antidote is to orient toward creation: add more value to the world than you take from it.
questions.section: Living a Purposeful Life — Create More than You Consume

questions.question: What is MM's simple arithmetic argument for working 80–100 hour weeks versus 40?
questions.short_answer: If someone else works 50 hours and you work 100, you accomplish in 4 months what takes them a year.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM frames extreme work hours not as suffering but as a compounding advantage. The math is stark: doubling your hours doesn't just help you keep up — it lets you lap the competition. He applies this especially to company-building, arguing that revolutionary products like electric cars or reusable rockets simply cannot be built on 40-hour weeks. He does, however, acknowledge in hindsight that occasionally pausing — 'smelling the roses' — has merit too.
questions.section: Living a Purposeful Life — Work Like Hell

questions.principle: Feel the Fear; Do It Anyway
questions.question: How does MM use fatalism to reduce fear when making high-risk decisions?
questions.short_answer: By fully accepting the true probability of failure upfront, so the worst-case scenario no longer holds power over him.
questions.elaborated_answer: When starting SpaceX, MM estimated less than a 10% chance of success and accepted he would likely lose everything. By genuinely accepting that outcome — rather than suppressing it — he neutralized the fear of failure. He calls this productive fatalism: once you have honestly stared down the worst case and decided the mission is still worth attempting, fear loses its grip and decision-making becomes clearer. He distinguishes this from recklessness — it's calculated, not blind.
questions.section: Living a Purposeful Life — Feel the Fear; Do It Anyway

questions.principle: Feel the Fear; Do It Anyway
questions.question: What does MM say you should do when everyone tells you an idea is crazy?
questions.short_answer: Feel the fear fully, let the importance of the mission drive you, and do it anyway.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM rejects the idea that courage means not feeling fear. He says he feels it strongly. The distinction he draws is that when something is important enough, you act in spite of fear rather than waiting for it to disappear. He also notes that the absence of fear would itself be a sign something is mentally wrong. His mantra in tough moments is not optimism or pessimism — it's simply: 'This must get done. We will do it or die trying.'
questions.section: Living a Purposeful Life — Feel the Fear; Do It Anyway

questions.principle: Seek the Nature of the Universe
questions.question: What is the 'philosophy of curiosity' MM describes as the foundation of his worldview?
questions.short_answer: Expand consciousness — by creating more humans and digital intelligence — to better understand the nature of reality and the meaning of life.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM holds that humanity is only 5,000 years into civilization (a blip on a million-year timeline) and cannot yet answer fundamental questions about existence. His solution is to increase the total scope of consciousness — more humans, more digital intelligence, more variety in thinking — so we become capable of asking the right questions of the universe. He draws on Douglas Adams's Hitchhiker's Guide: the universe is the answer; we need to figure out the right questions. This drives his push for Mars colonization and AI development.
questions.section: Living a Purposeful Life — Seek the Nature of the Universe

questions.question: What does MM mean when he says 'Physics is law. Everything else is a recommendation'?
questions.short_answer: The laws of physics are the only truly non-negotiable constraints — all other rules, conventions, and norms are open to challenge.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM uses physics as his baseline for what is truly fixed versus what is merely conventional. Human-made rules, industry norms, regulatory frameworks, and business conventions can be broken, ignored, or redesigned. The laws of physics cannot. This distinction is liberating: if something doesn't violate physics, it may be achievable — no matter how many people say it can't be done. It's a call to challenge every non-physical constraint ruthlessly while respecting the ones that are truly inviolable.
questions.section: Think Like a Physicist — Obsess Over Truth

questions.question: What cognitive trap does MM identify as the biggest cause of mistakes in business and personal life?
questions.short_answer: Wishful thinking — filtering out information that conflicts with what you want to be true.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM says wishful thinking is a natural human tendency but a dangerous one. The brain tends to accept information that confirms what it hopes for and reject information that doesn't. His antidote is obsessive truth-seeking: asking 'is this actually true?' rather than 'does this feel right?' He even applies this to himself by assuming he is losing even when things look good — a deliberate counterweight to the natural human bias toward optimism. Rigorous self-analysis, not positive thinking, is his tool.
questions.section: Think Like a Physicist — Obsess Over Truth

questions.question: What is the difference between reasoning by analogy and reasoning from first principles?
questions.short_answer: Analogy copies or iterates on what already exists; first principles rebuilds reasoning from the most fundamental, provable truths upward.
questions.elaborated_answer: Reasoning by analogy — 'we do it this way because that's how it's always been done' — is faster and easier, and MM says it's fine for most everyday decisions. But for important, novel problems, analogy traps you in incrementalism. First-principles thinking means stripping away assumptions until you reach foundational truths, then reasoning back up. It produces counterintuitive conclusions — like the realization that battery packs or rockets don't have to be expensive — that analogy-based thinking would never surface.
questions.section: Think Like a Physicist — First-Principles Thinking

questions.question: What is the 'Idiot Index' and how does MM use it?
questions.short_answer: The ratio of a finished product's cost to the cost of its raw materials — a high ratio signals needless complexity or manufacturing inefficiency.
questions.elaborated_answer: The Idiot Index emerged from SpaceX's first-principles analysis of rocket costs. MM asked: what are the raw material costs of a rocket? About 1–2% of the finished product price. That gap — a factor of 50–100x — reveals manufacturing inefficiency, over-engineered designs, or both. He expects his engineers to know the best and worst parts in their systems by this index at all times. A component costing $13,000 made of $200 of steel has a high Idiot Index — a clear target for redesign.
questions.section: Think Like a Physicist — First-Principles Thinking

questions.question: What physics tool does MM use to stress-test an idea by imagining it at extreme scales?
questions.short_answer: Thinking in limits — scaling an idea to a very large or very small number to see how it behaves and what becomes possible.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM borrows 'thinking in limits' from physics, where you push a variable to an extreme to reveal underlying structure. Applied to business and engineering, it means asking: if we produced a million units per year, would this still be expensive? If so, the issue is design, not volume. Or: if we could build 10 levels of tunnels underground, could we fully solve urban traffic? Thinking in limits reveals whether a constraint is fundamental or just a result of current scale, and opens up entirely new solution spaces.
questions.section: Think Like a Physicist — Thinking in Limits

questions.question: How does MM describe the 'theoretically perfect product' design approach?
questions.short_answer: Imagine the ideal arrangement of atoms for the perfect product, then work backwards to figure out how to get atoms into that shape.
questions.elaborated_answer: Rather than designing from the tools you currently have — which leads to products constrained by familiar methods — MM proposes a two-direction approach: design forward from existing capabilities AND design backward from the theoretically perfect version. The 'perfect product' is a moving target that updates as you learn, but approximating it forces you to ask: what tools, materials, or manufacturing methods would we need to invent? This prevents premature convergence on good-enough solutions.
questions.section: Think Like a Physicist — Thinking in Limits

questions.question: What does MM mean by 'aspire to be less wrong' rather than aspiring to be right?
questions.short_answer: Treat beliefs as provisional and continuously update them toward truth — the goal is reducing error over time, not achieving certainty.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM reframes the goal of thinking from 'being right' to 'being less wrong.' This matters because treating beliefs as fixed and correct leads to confirmation bias and resistance to new evidence. Treating them as working approximations that should improve with new data creates a mindset of constant calibration. He also adds: 'It's OK to be wrong. Just don't be confident and wrong.' Confidence without evidence is the dangerous combination — uncertainty paired with curiosity is productive.
questions.section: Think Like a Physicist — Aspire to Be Less Wrong

questions.question: Why does MM recommend viewing knowledge as a 'semantic tree'?
questions.short_answer: You must understand the trunk and main branches — fundamental principles — before learning the leaves (details), otherwise the details have nothing to hang on to.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM argues that learning without strong foundations leads to fragile, disconnected knowledge. Just as leaves on a tree need branches to support them, detailed knowledge needs to be anchored in core principles. This is why he advocates reading broadly — to build the overall map of knowledge — before specializing. It also explains his self-education approach: he read voraciously across physics, engineering, and history, building mental scaffolding before diving into the technical specifics of rocketry or battery chemistry.
questions.section: Think Like a Physicist — Aspire to Be Less Wrong

questions.question: What Arthur C. Clarke quote inspired MM to pursue engineering as a career?
questions.short_answer: 'A sufficiently advanced technology is indistinguishable from magic.'
questions.elaborated_answer: Clarke's quote crystallized something MM had felt since childhood: that advanced technology, to anyone outside the current era, looks like pure magic. Flying, instant global communication, accessing all human knowledge from a pocket device — these would have been incomprehensible to people 300 years ago, even beyond what they imagined possible. This framing made engineering aspirational and almost spiritual for MM: if you can advance technology, you become a magician. That is what he has pursued.
questions.section: The Value of Engineering — Engineering Is Magic

questions.question: What is the key distinction MM draws between science and engineering?
questions.short_answer: Science discovers what already exists in the universe; engineering creates things that have never existed before.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM deeply respects science but argues that engineering is the true lever of progress. Science is limited by available data — without new instruments and machines, you can only make deeper inferences from existing observations. Engineering generates the new data by building new tools (like Galileo's telescope), which in turn enables new scientific discoveries. Without engineering, physics hits a ceiling. The limiting factor in advancing civilization is almost always engineering, which is why MM spends 80% of his time on it.
questions.section: The Value of Engineering — Engineering Is Magic

questions.question: What does MM argue is missing from Sun Tzu's 'The Art of War'?
questions.short_answer: A chapter on technology — specifically, that a decisive technological advantage can win wars with minimal casualties.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM has read The Art of War many times and respects it, but notes a significant gap: it barely addresses technology. He argues that throughout history — from Roman metallurgy to WWII bombers to the nuclear bomb — technological superiority has been the decisive factor, often overriding numbers, tactics, and generalship entirely. In the modern era, wars are technology race wars. The side that innovates faster wins. The Manhattan Project is his clearest example: whoever got the nuclear bomb first, won — full stop.
questions.section: The Value of Engineering — Engineering Wins Wars

questions.question: What lesson does MM draw from the technological 'rock-paper-scissors' game of WWII fighter development?
questions.short_answer: What matters most is the pace of innovation — the side that iterates fastest ultimately wins.
questions.elaborated_answer: During WWII, every major power — the US, Japan, Germany, UK, Russia — kept producing aircraft to counter the other's latest design. Each new plane was an answer to the previous one. MM's takeaway is that no single design wins forever; what matters is who can develop and field new technology fastest. The US started WWII with terrible fighters and ended it dominating the skies. The lesson applies directly to business: sustained rapid iteration is more powerful than any single breakthrough.
questions.section: The Value of Engineering — Engineering Wins Wars

questions.question: Why does MM say product ideas are 'nearly irrelevant'?
questions.short_answer: Because ideas are abundant and easy — the hard, scarce, and valuable part is execution: building, manufacturing, and achieving positive cash flow.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM describes his own mind as having more ideas than he could ever execute. This makes ideas cheap. The true bottleneck — and therefore the source of real value — is execution: turning a concept into a working prototype, then into a manufacturable product, then into a cash-flow-positive business. He distinguishes sharply: 'Prototypes are easy; production is hard.' Real engineering value is created by solving the brutal, unglamorous problems of manufacturing at scale — not by conceiving the idea in the first place.
questions.section: The Value of Engineering — Engineering Creates Value

questions.question: How does MM use the fact that Toyota and Mercedes buy powertrains from Tesla as proof of Tesla's engineering credibility?
questions.short_answer: If electric powertrain engineering were easy, established automakers would do it themselves — the fact that they buy from Tesla proves Tesla solved genuinely hard problems.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM uses this as a first-principles proof: large, capable companies like Toyota, Daimler, and Mercedes have vast engineering resources. If electric powertrains were straightforward, they would build them in-house. The fact that they chose to buy from Tesla signals that Tesla solved something they couldn't. This is MM's general model for validating real value creation: if sophisticated buyers choose your product over doing it themselves, you've built something genuinely hard and genuinely valuable.
questions.section: The Value of Engineering — Engineering Creates Value
